rules: We are testing the product, not you. There are no wrong answers.
Please think out loud as you go — tell me what you are looking at and what you are thinking.
If you get stuck, tell me what you expected to happen.
I may stay quiet for a moment — that is intentional, not a sign you did anything wrong.
setup.noHelpPolicy: Wait 10 seconds before prompting. If stuck, ask: What would you do next if I was not here? Do not point to UI elements or explain what things do. If they ask is this right — say: What do you think?

groups.tabContext: Participant has not seen the product yet. No prototype on screen.

questions.label: Current workflow
questions.mainQuestion: Walk me through how you find where surgical costs are running high today. What do you actually do?
probingQuestions: What do you have to pull together to get there?
Who else gets involved?
questions.whatToDo: Let them answer fully without interrupting. Do not mention the product or any features.
questions.whatToListenFor: What triggers their attention. What data sources, reports, or people they rely on. Where the work actually starts.

questions.label: Where it breaks
questions.mainQuestion: In that process — where do you get stuck, or stop trusting what you are looking at?
probingQuestions: Has a number ever turned out wrong on you? What happened?
questions.whatToDo: Stay curious and neutral. Do not reassure them that the product solves this — yet.
questions.whatToListenFor: Trust gaps, data quality concerns, time waste, moments where the work stalls or gets handed off.

groups.tabContext: Show the Opportunities Hub with a single Supply card visible. Let them land on it without explanation.

questions.label: Cold read
questions.mainQuestion: Before I explain anything — what do you think this card is telling you?
probingQuestions: What on the card tells you that?
Who do you picture using this?
questions.whatToDo: Stay silent for at least 15 seconds. Do not explain any element. Note what they read first.
questions.whatToListenFor: Whether they grasp the supply variance story unaided. Which elements anchor their reading. Who they see as the user.

questions.label: The number
questions.mainQuestion: It says you could save 120 to 195 thousand. What does that number mean to you?
probingQuestions: Is that money already saved, or something else?
It is a range, not one figure. Does that help you or bother you?
questions.whatToDo: Do not define the number. Let them interpret it in their own terms first.
questions.whatToListenFor: Whether they read it as realized vs potential. Whether the range reads as honest or vague. Whether they want one number to anchor on.

questions.label: The pairwise comparison
questions.mainQuestion: This card puts one high-cost provider next to one low-cost provider. What is your honest reaction to comparing them that way?
probingQuestions: What would you need to know before you trusted that those two are a fair match?
The providers show as codes, not names. Does that work for you?
questions.whatToDo: Do not defend the pairing. Let them push on it if they want to.
questions.whatToListenFor: Case mix, acuity, payer, volume — what they need to feel the comparison is fair. Comfort or discomfort with anonymized provider codes.

questions.label: What is missing
questions.mainQuestion: If you were going to act on this card — what is not here that you would need first?
probingQuestions: What would you check before you brought this to anyone?
questions.whatToDo: This is a generative question. Do not prompt with suggestions. Let the silence pull more out.
questions.whatToListenFor: Specific gaps — patient mix, time period, who else they would loop in, what they would verify before sharing.

groups.tabContext: Navigate to the Procedure Recommendations view. One procedure aggregating multiple opportunities, with the cost drivers panel and the benchmark slider visible.

questions.label: Does the aggregation land
questions.mainQuestion: Now everything for one procedure is in one place. Is this how you would want to think about it — or would you rather stay with the single cards?
probingQuestions: Where would you look first on this screen?
questions.whatToDo: Let them orient before asking. Watch their eye path.
questions.whatToListenFor: Whether the procedure-level frame matches how they reason. Whether they miss the granularity of single cards.

questions.label: The recommendation
questions.mainQuestion: Read this recommendation. Does it feel like the app is telling you what to do, or showing you what it found?
probingQuestions: Which of those would you trust more?
Is there anything it does not know about your world that it should?
questions.whatToDo: Let them sit with the wording. Do not paraphrase the recommendation for them.
questions.whatToListenFor: Tone perception — prescriptive vs evidentiary. What context they feel the system is missing about their setting.

questions.label: Cost drivers panel
questions.mainQuestion: This panel breaks the cost into drivers — supplies, length of stay, pharmacy, and so on. Now that you have seen it — is this useful to you, or just more detail?
probingQuestions: Clicking a driver filters the recommendations below it. Does that connection make sense?
If you had to start somewhere, would this panel change where you start?
questions.whatToDo: Briefly explain the panel and the click-to-filter behaviour, then ask. Do not click through it for them.
questions.whatToListenFor: Whether drivers map to how they already break down cost. Whether the filtering interaction is discoverable and meaningful.

questions.label: The benchmark
questions.mainQuestion: There is a benchmark you can set — Easy, Moderate, Hard. Looking at it, which setting is the tougher target to hit?
probingQuestions: Where do you think that benchmark number comes from?
Would you trust the one the app picked, or set your own?
questions.whatToDo: Do not explain the percentile logic. See if the labels are self-evident.
questions.whatToListenFor: Whether Easy and Hard read in the expected direction. Trust in a system-set benchmark vs desire for control.

questions.label: The moving line
questions.mainQuestion: When you change the benchmark, the green line moves and the list of high-cost and low-cost providers shifts with it. Walk me through what that change is telling you.
probingQuestions: A harder benchmark leaves more providers above the line. Does that match what you would expect?
Does seeing the low-cost providers below the line help you, or is it just extra?
questions.whatToDo: Have them move the slider themselves. Watch whether they connect the line, the list, and the benchmark setting.
questions.whatToListenFor: Whether they grasp the linkage. Whether low-cost providers register as a useful reference point or as noise.

groups.tabContext: Show the Snapshot screen — the first screen of the app, splitting the view into Procedures, Providers, and Cost Drivers.

questions.label: Ten-second read
questions.mainQuestion: In about ten seconds — what is this screen trying to tell you?
probingQuestions: What is the headline here?
questions.whatToDo: Time them silently. Do not point at anything.
questions.whatToListenFor: Whether the page communicates a single message or feels like a wall of widgets.

questions.label: The split
questions.mainQuestion: Procedures, Providers, Cost Drivers. Does splitting it up that way match how you think — or would you cut it differently?
probingQuestions: Is anything filed in a place you would not expect?
questions.whatToDo: Let them critique freely. Do not justify the structure.
questions.whatToListenFor: Mental model fit. Items that feel misfiled. Categories they would add or merge.

questions.label: Does the overview earn its place
questions.mainQuestion: This is the first screen someone opens, before the detail. Would you actually start your work here — or go straight past it to the cards?
probingQuestions: What would this screen need to be worth stopping on?
questions.whatToDo: This is a hard question about value. Do not flinch from a negative answer.
questions.whatToListenFor: Whether the overview adds value or is treated as a pass-through. What specifically would make it sticky.

groups.tabContext: Close or minimize the prototype. Closing questions only — no screen needed.

questions.label: The forced cut
questions.mainQuestion: If the team could keep only one of the three tabs you saw — which one stays? And what do you lose without the other two?
probingQuestions: What made the other two easier to give up?
One last thing — what is one thing you held back from saying? (only if the room feels honest, then stop and do not fill the silence)
questions.whatToDo: Let the forced choice do the work. After the optional last probe, stop talking. Do not fill the silence.
questions.whatToListenFor: The tab that earns its place under pressure. What they regret losing. Anything held back that surfaces in the silence.
